coredata.dc:title: Preparation of multi-defect state photo-responsive N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs materials and study of their properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Yi
Wang, Luhua
Zhao, Hongfang
Li, Hongxia
Niu, Xiaohui
Chen, Li
Zhang, Deyi
Fan, Haiyan
Wang, Kunjie
coredata.dc:description: The imperative detection and elimination of hexavalent Cr(VI), a potent environmental pollutant and living organism hazard, is paramount for ecological conservation. The precipitation technique and charge action principles were used in this work to synthesis a cost-effective multi-defect nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/copper sulfide/carbon nitride composite photo-responsive materials (N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs, N-GCC) with good performance. To improve Cr(VI) detection and removal efficiency, multiple-point defects were generated to optimize the material's electrical structure and photo-responsivity. The multi-point defects were proved to greatly improve the light absorption of N-GCC, photosensitivity and consequently, the recognition rate of Cr(VI). The electron-rich multi-point defect structure accelerated the electrostatic adsorption and photoreduction of Cr(VI), which made the rapid removal of high Cr(VI) concentration possible, and it could remove 95.76 % of 120 mg/L Cr(VI) in 50 min with the specific surface area of N-GCC of 28.81 m2/g only. This study reveals the great potential of multi-point defect structures in treating Cr(VI) heavy metal pollution and opens up a new pathway for the remediation of chromium-contaminated environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cr(VI)
Surface defect engineering
Adsorption-photocatalytic synergy
PEC detection
Doping

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundations of China (Grant No. 22065021), Lanzhou Talent Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project (Grant Nos. 2022-RC-33), Hongliu Excellent Youth Teacher Cultivate Project of Lanzhou University of Technology.

Yi Ren and Luhua Wang contributed equally to this work. This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundations of China (Grant No. 22065021), Lanzhou Talent Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project (Grant Nos. 2022-RC-33), Hongliu Excellent Youth Teacher Cultivate Project of Lanzhou University of Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N-GQDs, CuS/Cv-CNNs-70, N-GCC-2 of (a) XRD, (b) FT-IR, N-GCC-2 of (c) XPS, (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s, (f) Cu 2p, (g) S 2p, (h) O 1s spectra.
SEM images of (a) CuS/Cv-CNNs-70, (b) N-GCC-0.5, (c) N-GCC-1, (d) N-GCC-2 (1 μm), (e) N-GCC-3, (f) N-GCC-2 (200 nm).
(a) TEM, (b–e) HR-TEM, and (f) electron-selected diffractograms of N-GCC-2.

(a)shows the UV–vis spectra, CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 tauc plots, N-GCC-2-X tauc plots, and Mott-Schottky curves. (d) N-GCC-2 VB-XPS plot (e) CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 and N-GCC-2 photocurrent response in visible light; (f) EIS spectra of different materials; (g) Schematic constructions; and (f) PL spectra of different materials.

(a) Effect of pH on the ability of N-GCC-2 to remove Cr(VI), (b) adsorption and photocatalytic capacity of different materials for the removal of Cr(VI), (c) synergistic removal of Cr(VI) by adsorption and photocatalysis of N-GCC-2, (d) UV–vis spectra of Commercially available CuS, (e) UV–vis spectra of CuS/Cv-CNNs-70, and (f) UV–vis spectra of N-GCC-2.

N-GCC-2 and Commercially available CuS. (a) Adsorption time profile, (b) Pseudo-first-order kinetic model, (c) Pseudo-second-order kinetic model, (d) Adsorption profiles for different concentrations of Cr(VI), (e) Langmuir adsorption isothermal model, (f) Freundlich adsorption isothermal model.
(a) Effect of incubation time, (b) concentration, (c) linearity, (d) pH, (e) interfering ions, and (f) number of cycles on the recognition of Cr(VI) by N-GCC-2.
Photocatalytic mechanism of N-GCC-2 for Cr(VI).
Kinetic parameters of Cr(VI) adsorption on N-GCC-2.
Adsorption isotherm parameters of Cr(VI) on N-GCC-2.

head.ce:title: Preparation of multi-defect state photo-responsive N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs materials and study of their properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, 730050, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Nazarbayev University, Astana, 010000, Kazakhstan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Nazarbayev University, Astana 010000, Kazakhstan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The imperative detection and elimination of hexavalent Cr(VI), a potent environmental pollutant and living organism hazard, is paramount for ecological conservation. The precipitation technique and charge action principles were used in this work to synthesis a cost-effective multi-defect nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/copper sulfide/carbon nitride composite photo-responsive materials (N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs, N-GCC) with good performance. To improve Cr(VI) detection and removal efficiency, multiple-point defects were generated to optimize the material's electrical structure and photo-responsivity. The multi-point defects were proved to greatly improve the light absorption of N-GCC, photosensitivity and consequently, the recognition rate of Cr(VI). The electron-rich multi-point defect structure accelerated the electrostatic adsorption and photoreduction of Cr(VI), which made the rapid removal of high Cr(VI) concentration possible, and it could remove 95.76 % of 120 mg/L Cr(VI) in 50 min with the specific surface area of N-GCC of 28.81 m2/g only. This study reveals the great potential of multi-point defect structures in treating Cr(VI) heavy metal pollution and opens up a new pathway for the remediation of chromium-contaminated environments.

Synthesizing the advantages of surface defect engineering, a heterojunction material of N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs containing different point defects with significant photocatalytic and adsorption capabilities as well as strong photoelectrochemical signals is proposed to enhance the comprehensive treatment for Cr(VI). The construction of multifocal defects suppresses the complexation of photogenerated carriers on N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs, resulting in a strong removal capacity efficiency for the anion Cr(VI) as well as a high detection capability.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution has been a persistent threat to human society. In particular, emissions of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) from industrial processes have been strictly regulated due to their irreversible damage to health such as nasal septum perforation, respiratory allergic reactions, skin inflammation, allergic contact dermatitis, and a significant carcinogenic risk [1,2]. The challenges brought by Cr(VI) emissions make it valuable to develop some techniques in environmental monitoring and remediation of chromium-containing wastewater.

Liquid and gas chromatography,3 spectroscopic analysis,4 mass spectrometry by inductively coupled plasma (ICP-MS),5 electroanalysis,6 and spectroscopic analysis are now the most widely used methods for the measurement of Cr(VI). These methods all allow for the quick identification of Cr(VI). Nevertheless, the aforementioned methods suffer from the factors such as background noise interference, the bulkiness of the equipment, and cumbersome sample preprocessing steps [3,4]. Because of its proven superiority in biological and environmental studies, photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing technology has garnered a lot of interest [5]. PEC sensors not only share most common advantages of electrochemical analysis, such as cost-effectiveness, ease operation, and high signal-to-noise ratio [6], but also offer significant advantages over fluorescence sensing [7]. It operates by transferring and separating photogenerated electrons at the material-electrolyte contact, generating a detectable signal. Therefore, enhancing the photo-responsiveness of the material is the key to ensuring the performance of PEC sensors [8].

For the treatment of chromium-containing wastewater, the more widely used methods include chemical reduction [9], precipitation [10], ion exchange [11], membrane separation [11], and biological methods [12]. Adsorption coupled with photocatalysis has been applied to remove Cr(VI) greatly reducing the cost and processing time. More precisely, this action accelerates the extremely dangerous Cr(VI) adsorption onto the surface of the photocatalyst and further decreases it to the easily precipitated, low-toxicity form of Cr(III).

Known for its exceptional photo-responsive properties, copper (II) sulfide (CuS) offers visible light response, long-term separation of photogenerated carriers, and versatility in a variety of chemical processes [13]. The aforementioned characteristics of CuS extend its applications in environmental purification, energy conversion, and organic synthesis. Nevertheless, relatively low light absorption capacity of CuS, lack of a photogenerated carrier separation pathway, the susceptibility of photogenerated electron and hole pairs (e−-h+) to complexation [14], as well as the absence of catalytic sites lead to its underutilization in treating real pollution. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) was recently added to the composites to change the properties of light absorption, lengthen the lifetime of photogenerated carriers, diversify the transportation route, and improve the efficiency of photovoltaic conversion and energy conversion [15]. On the other hand, N-GQDs, as quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials derived from graphene, have been recogonized with their excellent conductivity, adjustable energy gap, and UPCL [16]. These properties make N-GQDs a great option for modifications that will increase the catalysts' activity and photocatalytic stability, promote the migration and separation of carriers produced by photosynthesis, and accelerate the rate of light absorption. Compounding these three materials and incorporating them into industrial wastewater treatment systems, particularly for Cr(VI)-containing wastewater, is expected to significantly improve Cr(VI) removal efficiency, allowing for large-scale water detection and purification using solar energy or artificial light sources.

Point defects on the surface have the potential to modify the charge distribution and arrangement of the atoms on the surface.21 This modification can increase the substrate's affinity for pollutant moieties, leading to a higher concentration of contaminants on the catalyst's interface. Consequently, this can improve the purification process' effectiveness. Target substance detection in photoelectrochemical (PEC) detection may be made very selective and sensitive by optimizing the material's photoelectric characteristics and surface reactivity by the inclusion of certain flaws [17]. For example, Zhao et al. introduced oxygen vacancies in Bi4O5I2 by a light-assisted approach, which significantly enhanced its PEC performance [18]. Reduced interfacial resistance and increased energy band bending caused by oxygen vacancies efficiently encourage the migration and separation of photoexcited carriers, producing greater and more stable photocurrent densities while inhibiting electron and hole recombination. This material thus shows excellent sensitivity and stability in PEC detection.

Recent research has shown that adding multiple defects to photo-responsive materials has improved the sensitivity and efficiency of treating wastewater containing chromium. This is because the addition of multiple defects increases the materials' capacity to absorb light, modifies their electronic structure and electron affinity, improves the surface active sites, and increases their photostability and ability to separate charges generated by light [19]. For example, Duan et al. fabricated a photodetector, whose photo-response was expanded from the visible to the infrared based on Ni atom-modified MoS2 with S vacancies [20], and a detectability of 8.9 × 109 Jones were achieved. Through the synthesis via chemical vapor deposition, Zhao and colleagues engineered MoSe2 featuring V dopants and molybdenum vacancy defects [21]. The study found that the electrical transport properties, specifically charge carrier mobility, were augmented proportionally to the vanadium dopant density. Additionally, the fabricated photodetector exhibited an extremely broad spectral detection range spanning from ultraviolet to near-infrared wavelengths (365 nm–2240 nm), with notable peak responsivities measured at 9700 mA/W for 520 nm and 2800 mA/W at 2240 nm, respectively.

The present work aims to synthesize N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs (N-GCC) composites electrostatic interactions with significant photo-responsiveness [22]. A considerable number of multi-defects were found in N-GCC, which are mostly in charge of Cr(VI)'s strong photoelectric recognition. Nitrogen doping of N-GQDs effectively enhances adsorption performance by increasing the active sites, surface area, and pore structure, more availability for chemical bonds, and modulating the surface charge, thus accelerating the removal of Cr(VI). The design of the composite material contributes to the development of multifunctional composites for not only Cr(VI) removal, but also for the treatment of other heavy metal ions and organic pollutants, which is expected to realize the overall purification of water bodies. By investigating the regeneration and recycling capabilities of the material, its low treatment cost could improve the economics of practical applications.
Standards for analytical purity are met by all substances. For more information, see "Supporting Information".
The preparation of all materials was carried out at ambient temperature and pressure except for the temperatures already communicated.
CuS/Cv-CNNs were created based on prior research (details may be found in "Supporting Information").
By using earlier research, we created N-GQDs (see "Supporting Information" for specifics).
0.098 g of CuS/Cv-CNNs with 0.002 g of N-GQDs were placed in 45 mL of ethanol and the mixed solution was stirred for 12 h. After the ethanol was evaporated, the powder was dried at 80 °C.
(For specifics, see the accompanying documentation).

Photoelectrochemical characterization was conducted using a Shanghai Chenhua CHI660E system within a standard tri-electrode assembly. The photocatalyst, 9 mg, was evenly suspended in 240 μL of ethylene glycol and 10 μL of 5 wt% Nafion solution to create the photoactive material. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min to create a homogenized colloidal ink.Then, an ITO (indium tin oxide) substrate was covered with 50 μL of this dispersion, defining the working electrode (WE) as a 2 × 1 cm area. The electrolytic medium consisted of 0.5 M Na2SO4. Illumination is provided by a 50 W LED light source with spectral irradiance limited to wavelengths above 420 nm. Meanwhile, an open-circuit voltage range of 10 mHz–100 kHz was used for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with a 5 mV disturbance amplitude. Photocurrent measurements were conducted at ambient circumstances without an applied bias. Finally, Mott-Schottky plots were acquired between −1 V and 0.5 V at open-circuit potentials.

A homemade PEC device was used to perform all photoelectrochemical (PEC) tests. The prepared electrodes were initially immersed in a Cr(VI) solution for a while before being rinsed three times with deionized water to remove any residue. The dried samples were retained for subsequent experiments. The light source was an LED bulb with a power of 30 W. Using a CHI660E electrochemical station with a 2 cm2 surface area for the working electrode (N-GCC-2), a Pt sheet for the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl for the control electrode, photocurrent was measured. Every measurement of photocurrent was conducted using a bias voltage of 0.5 V. As the electrolyte, 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution was employed. An LED light (30 W, 400 ≤ λ < 600 nm) was used to illuminate the photocurrent measurements while the PEC test was conducted at room temperature.
Cr(VI) was subjected to photoreduction while illuminated by an LED light bulb (50 W, λ > 420 nm). Add 30 mg of photocatalyst to 100 mL of Cr(VI) aqueous solution at a rate of 120 mg/L.

The photocatalytic reduction efficiency was assessed over a pH range from 1 to 6. The adsorptive contributions were minimized by dark adsorption for 60 min before photonic activation. By putting it via a 0.45 μm polypropylene syringe filter, the photocatalyst was removed from the process. The residual Cr(VI) was quantitatively analyzed using the 1,5-diphenylcarbazide spectrophotometric method at 540 nm.
(For specifics, see the accompanying documentation).
(For specifics, see the accompanying documentation).

CuS/Cv-CNNs-70's XRD diffraction pattern (Fig. 1 a) shows distinct peaks at 2θ values of 13.0°, 27.4°, 29.2°, 31.7°, 32.7°, 47.9°, 52.8°, and 59.4°. These peaks can be identified as the (100) and (002) lattice planes of Cv-CNNs and the (102), (103), (106), (110), (108), and (116) crystal planes of CuS, respectively (JCPDS, No. 060464) [24,25]. Such evidence supports the effective synthesis of CuS/Cv-CNNs-70. The observed shift to a greater 2θ angle compared to pure compounds suggests the establishment of a heterojunction, which is crucial for reducing interplanar space and preventing lattice deformation [26]. The diffraction pictures of N-GQDs demonstrate their amorphous nature, 32 in agreement with the conclusions drawn from the literature. The diffraction patterns exhibited all of the CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 framework's diffraction characteristics, as well as bigger diffraction peaks when N-GQD was complexed with CuS/Cv-CNNs-70, indicating that the composite was developing.

FTIR spectrum CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 (Fig. 1b) a wide absorption band at 3400 cm−1, which is indicative of the Cv-CNNs moieties' stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H. The characteristic peaks at 1641 cm−1, 1568 cm−1 and 1322 cm−1 correspond to the CO stretching, N–H bending vibration, and CN stretching vibration respectively [27], a typical vibrational spectroscopic features for amide functional group. The vibrational characterization of the tri-s-triazine molecule in the structure of Cv-CNNs produces a unique absorption band at about 805 cm−1, which suggests that the C–N bond in the triazine molecule is undergoing a specific stretching vibration. The symmetry of the molecular structure is broken and the vibrational frequency is lowered, leading to a shift of the absorption band towards lower energies, which was originally at 810 cm−1 redshifted to 805 cm−1, confirming the introduction of a carbon vacancy [28]. A weak band at 610 cm−1 can be identified as Cu–S stretching vibration implying the successful composite of the CuS. In contrast, a wide absorption signature for N-CQDs is seen in the 3000 cm−1 to 3350 cm−1 range, and it is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C–H, O–H, and N–H. The CO stretching vibration in the carboxylic group is responsible for the absorption band recorded at 1725 cm−1, whereas the CO stretching in the amide group is responsible for the band noticed at 1648 cm−1 [29].

The survey XPS spectrum of N-GCC-2 (Fig. 1c) shows recognizable peaks for Cu 2p, O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and S 2p. Distinct binding energy peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.8 eV are visible in the high resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. 1d), which correspond to the C in the forms of C–C, C–O, and N–CN, respectively [30,31]. For the N 1s spectra (Fig. 1e), three peaks at 398.1 eV, 399.4 eV, and 400.9 eV are ascribed to the binding form of N in C–N–C, CN–C, and C–N–H, respectively, in Cv-CNNs and N-GQDs [32]. Two types of Cu can be seen in the XPS spectrum of Cu 2p (Fig. 1f). Cu 2p in CuS is represented by two peaks at 932.5 eV and 952.3 eV, whereas Cu 2p in CuO is represented by two peaks at 934.6 eV and 953.6 eV [33]. Two distinct peaks at binding energies of 162 eV and 163.7 eV, corresponding to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbitals,41 are revealed upon examination of the S 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 1g). On the other hand, the peak observed at 168.3 eV and 168.9 eV for N-GCC-2 corresponds to the SO4 2− a form of S [34]. For O 1s (Fig. 1h), two peaks at 532.1 eV and 533.0 eV are seen, indicating the addition of –OH to the composite.

In order to study the thermal stability of N-GCC-2, thermogravimetric analysis was carried out. Fig. S1 shows that in the lower temperature interval (35–210 °C), adsorbed moisture and volatile organic compounds in the composite evaporate, resulting in a small amount of mass loss. Losses at this stage are typically small, around 5 %.N-GQDs are carbon-based materials that undergo pyrolysis between 210 and 350 °C, accompanied by the onset of decomposition of Cv-CNNs (structural destabilization due to the introduction of carbon vacancies by Cv-CNNs). The mass loss at this stage is about 20 %, which mainly comes from the thermal decomposition of Cv-CNNs and N-GQDs. At 350–650 °C, the CuS also starts to decompose gradually due to the structural instability caused by the introduction of more defects, and the mass loss is about 32 %. Considering the limitations of the decomposition temperature, it is impossible to reach such high temperatures in practical applications, so N-GCC-2 is usually able to withstand the actual working temperature, and its excellent thermal stability ensures its long and stable operation in the reaction.

The SEM image of CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 (Fig. 2 a) shows a porous lamellar structure, which is caused by the releasing of NH3 gas from the decomposition of NH4Cl during two thermal polymerization processes [35]. Particles of CuS are loaded on the porous surface to adsorb the pollutants. Large surface-to-volume ratios, such as those seen in binary composites, enhance the transfer of photogenerated charge, speeding up the migration of photogenerated carriers to the external circuit and reducing the chance of carrier composites. In the N-GCC-70-X composites (Fig. 2b–f), there are no immediately visible N-GQDs due to their smaller size; 44 instead, the morphology is similar to that of CuS/Cv-CNNs-70.

CuS nanocrystals are equally distributed throughout the Cv-CNN lamellar structures, as evidenced by the TEM picture of the N-GCC-2 composite (Fig. 3 a). This helps to create the heterojunction structure, which allows for the effective separation and migration of photogenerated charges. 45The HR-TEM picture (Fig. 3b) shows some homogeneous N-GQD particles, suggesting their composite with the substrate material. Both the lattice fringing and electron-selective diffraction patterns of the composite materials confirm their polycrystalline character.

Owing to a multi-defect mechanism, a new energy barrier is formed at the N-GCC-X Fermi energy level leading to the emergence of localized electronic states [36]. Consequently, in the 200–700 nm wavelength range, N-GCC-2 shown greater optical absorption capabilities than CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 (Fig. 4 a). Because N-CQDs have a high visible light absorption efficiency [37], the composite involving N-CQDs boosts the light absorption for the whole system. Subsequently, the forbidden bandwidths were determined using equ. (1):
(1)
α h v = A
(
h v −
E g
)
n / 2
where
α
represents absorption coefficient,
A
, proportionality constant,
E g
, band gap, and
n
can take the value of 1 or 4 depending on the conversion mode. The values of
E g
were calculated as 2.10 eV, 1.90 eV, 2.00 eV, 2.15 eV, and 1.80 eV for CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 and N-GCC-X, respectively (Fig. 4b). Despite of smaller bandwidth than that of N-GCC-X due to the presence of multi-point defects [38], which trap non-equilibrium carriers, CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 faces more challenges to separate e-h + pairs. While still belong to the category of narrow-band semiconductor, N-GCC-X indicates some advantages to separate e−-h+ pairs. Moreover, N-GCC-X is able to absorb more visible light regardless of its relatively larger bandwidth. Such a property sustains the photoinduced e−-h+ pairs and stops them from recombining. Mott-Schottky curves serve as a common method to determine the photocatalyst material type and energy band structure [39]. The positive slope of 1/C−2 versus applied potential indicates that all materials are n-type semiconductors with electronic conductivity. The values of
E
A g / A g C l
for CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 and N-GCC-X are −0.600 V, −0.365 V, −0.180 V, −0.090 V, and −0.375 V, respectively. For n-type semiconductors, the conduction band (CB) potential surpasses the
E
A g / A g C l
by approximately 0.197 V relative to the NHE, with variations contingent upon the semiconductor's carrier density and activity. Therefore, the CB and VB were calculated for different materials according to Eqs. (2) and (3) [40].
(2)
E
N H E
=
E
A g / A g C l
+ 0.197
(3)
E
V B
=
E
C B
+
E g

The Mott-Schottky curves for the different materials showed that the N-GCC-2 was able to produce more ·OH (Figs. S2a–c). N-GQDs serve as a pool of electrons, being able to capture the photogenerated e− from the CB of both CuS and Cv-CNNs. Consequently, the ternary composite involving N-CQDs effectively elongates the spatial separation of photogenerated h+ in the valence band (VB), promoting the generation of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) through the reaction with OH− in aqueous environments. The initiation of Fermi level bending at the ternary complex interfaces is a consequence of e−-h+ separation [41]. As a consequence, the VB and CB of N-GCC-2 are between those of N-GQDs, Cv-CNNs, and CuS, respectively. This type of energy alignment contributes to the stability of photogenerated e−-h+ couples. The results reveal that increasing the number of point defects in N-GCC-2 reduces the formation of protons, allowing photogenerated e− to engage with surface Cr (VI) and convert it to Cr (III). Furthermore, nitrogen doping in N-GQDs increases the energy level of VB while decreasing the band gap of N-GCC-2, hence improving light energy use efficiency.

In investigations involving transient photocurrent response, both CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 and N-GCC-2 exhibited a fast response to light (Fig. 4e). By testing the photocurrent density, it was found that the photocurrent density of N-GCC-2 reached 27.50 μA cm2, which is about 2.00 times higher than that of CuS/Cv-CNNs-70. This significant enhancement can be attributed to the fact that the photogenerated electrons (e−) generated in the CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 are efficiently captured by the N-GQDs in the composite and are rapidly transferred to the external circuits before the e−-h+ pairs undergo composite. Therefore, N-GCC-2 has better photocurrent response stability and structural stability due to the creation of electron acceptors and the introduction of point defects, which greatly increase the electron transport efficiency. The EIS spectra of CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 and N-GCC-X (Fig. 4f) showed that N-GCC-2 had the lowest charge transfer resistance at the solution interface, indicating the fastest e--h + separation and transport. Therefore, N-GCC-2 will show superior performance in Cr(VI) degradation.

As demonstrated in Fig. 4h, the PL intensity of N-GCC-2 is lower than that of CuS/Cv-CNNs-70 (Fig. 4h), implying that N-GQDs stabilize photogenerated electrons and extend their longevity, which is particularly desired to ensure a high photocatalytic efficiency.

The composite of N-GCC-2 exhibits a strong adsorptive removal of Cr (VI) in acidic environments. The best adsorption and photocatalytic activity was achieved at pH = 2, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The N-GCC-2 composite's isoelectric point allows positive charges to accumulate on its surface at low pH conditions, explaining its extraordinary adsorption and photocatalytic efficiency. The positive charge evolves a strong affiliation with Cr2O7 2− driven by an electrostatic dominated interaction. On the other hand, severe acidic conditions, particularly at pH < 2, greatly reduce photocatalytic effectiveness because H+ heavily corrodes N-GCC-2, which in turn reduces Cu2+ activity.

The elimination of Cr (VI) by N-GCC-70-X (Fig. 5b) rises as N-GQD incorporation increases. The reason for this increase in Cr(VI) removal is that the Fermi energy level changed as the composites were forming [42]. However, the N-GCC-70-3 composite exceeds the optimum threshold, reducing the availability of active sites and hence their capacity to attract Cr(VI) via electrostatics.

With an initial Cr(VI) concentration up to 120 mg/L, the N-GCC-2 composite showed a Cr(VI) removal effectiveness of 96.6 % under photonic activation, compared to 27.9 % for commercially available CuS. The importance of the composite catalyst was shown by the fact that in an independent test, where the spontaneous degradation of Cr(VI) under the identical light conditions was carried out without catalyst, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) was only 5.69 %.

It was demonstrated that the N-GCC-X composite's simultaneous adsorption-photocatalytic process enhanced the Cr(VI) elimination efficiency in a synergistic manner. First, when the number of N-CQDs incorporated grows to x = 2, the adsorption rate of Cr(VI) rises as well (Fig. 5a and b).Secondly, N-GCC-2 showed the highest overall removal rate (Fig. 5c) (95.76 %), which was higher than that of N-GCC-0 and N-GCC-1, and much higher than the performance of the monomer material (Fig. S3). UV–vis spectra (Fig. 5d–g) indicate a direct conversion from Cr(VI) to Cr(III) without showing any intermediate within 50 min, wherein, N-GCC-2 exhibits the most efficient removal rate of Cr(VI). This is attributed to the distribution of defective states within the N-GCC. Carbon vacancies are present in the g-C₃N₄ structure and affect the photovoltaic properties of the material by forming localized energy levels that act as trap states, trapping free carriers (electrons or holes). Sulfur vacancies are mainly found in CuS and affect the conductivity and light absorption of CuS by introducing defect states in the band gap of CuS, which may form shallow energy level trap states and affect electron transport. Copper vacancies can form deep energy level defect states in the band gap. These deep energy level defect states usually severely affect the carrier lifetime and mobility, leading to carrier capture during transport. Nitrogen doping improves the optoelectronic performance mainly by replacing carbon atoms, introducing new energy levels, changing the bandgap structure, and promoting charge separation and transport. The joint action between them goes to improve the removal efficiency of Cr(VI).

N-GCC-2's adsorptive performance (Fig. 6 a) demonstrates a quick absorption of Cr(VI) during the first 10 min, a progressive asymptotic approach to equilibrium, and a maximum sorption efficacy of 351.48 mg/g. The adsorption of Cr(VI) by N-GCC-2 is shown by kinetic analysis to follow a pseudo-second-order kinetic model (Fig. 6b–c), with R2 values reaching up to 0.999. Table 1 presents a summary of the fitting findings.

Isothermal adsorption investigations (Fig. 6d) reveal the adsorption capacity for Cr(VI) on N-GCC-2 follows both Temkin and Freundlich isothermal adsorption line models with good R2 (Table 2 ). Relatively, Temkin isotherm model, exhibits the superior coefficient of determination (R2), therefore, better represents the adsorption behavior of N-GCC-2 toward Cr(VI). The Temkin isotherm model confirms the strong affiliation between N-GCC-2 and Cr(VI).

Utilizing 3D response surface methodology, the interplay between pH levels and contact time was quantitatively optimized regarding their impact to adsorptive capacity (Fig.s S4a-d). The adsorptive uptake of Cr(VI) increases with the progressive acidification of the milieu. Meanwhile, the adsorptive uptake of Cr(VI) exhibited a positive correlation with time. The congruence between observed and model-predicted values and the residuals' normal probability plot support the predictive model's statistical soundness. The results showed that the appropriate pH and adsorption time had a positive effect on the adsorption amount.

In order to investigate the adsorption mechanism of N-GCC, BET tests were performed. By comparing the specific surface area of N-GCC-2 before and after adsorption (Fig. S5), it can be found that the specific surface area of N-GCC-2 before adsorption was about 28.8088 m2/g with a large pore size of about 70–920 nm or so, whereas the specific surface area of N-GCC-2 after adsorption was about 18.7120 m2/g with a pore size of about 70–800 nm. This shift in specific surface area was ascribed to the adsorption of Cr(VI) on N-GCC-2, which altered both its pore size and its own weight. As a result, N-GCC's specific surface area and pore size underwent a considerable change following the adsorption of Cr(VI). Notably, its smaller specific surface area (only 28.81 m2/g) suggests that other adsorption mechanisms may exist in addition to conventional pore adsorption. Combined with the results of our previous study (Fig. 5a), it can be found that the test of the effect of pH indicates that there may also be electrostatic attraction adsorption between N-GCC for Cr(VI), which was also confirmed in our previous study. In addition, the amino and carboxyl groups carried by the N-GQDs themselves can also serve as adsorption sites to promote the adsorption of Cr(VI).

With an increase in incubation time, N-GCC-2's recognition efficiency for Cr(VI) rises and achieves saturation after 30 min (Fig. 7 a). However, too long incubation time leads to partial detachment of N-GCC-2 from the ITO glass surface, resulting in a weaker photoelectric signal and a decrease in recognition efficiency.

The correlation between photocurrent intensity of the N-GCC-2 for recognizing Cr(VI) with concentration (Fig. 7b–c) indicates the maximum recognition rate with an ICr/I as high as 2.747 is achieved at a concentration of 150 μM of Cr(VI).Strong electrostatic interactions between N-GCC-2 and Cr(VI) at low concentrations cause the recognition rate to rise with concentration, while at higher concentrations, Cr(VI) covers N-GCC-2, which inhibits light absorption and also suppresses further adsorption of Cr(VI) due to increased electrostatic repulsion [43].

The pH effect (Fig. 7d) shows that in the neutral condition, N-GCC-2 is most capable of recognizing Cr(VI), viz., exhibits highest ICr/I value, due to multiple defects. The photocatalyst corrodes and part of the metal Cu2+ separates from the base material in acidic or alkaline environments, reducing the composite's sensitivity to light. The adsorptive capacity of N-GCC-2 for Cr(VI) ions was augmented in acidic media. In the meanwhile, in acidic conditions, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect significantly improved the composite's photonic absorbance properties. However, the destructive effect of H+ on the photocatalyst was greater than the enhancement of the light signal, which greatly limits the detection capability of Cr(VI).

The N-GCC-2 composite also indicates superior anti-interference-properties, enabling the precise discrimination of Cr(VI) species in the presence of various interfering ions (Fig. 7e). It is noteworthy that N-GCC-2 also possess certain recognition abilities for Fe3+ and Cu2+.

The ITO glass was dried and re-tested repeatedly in an electrolyte with Cr(VI) added to evaluate the stability of N-GCC-2 (Fig. 7f). After five cycles, the material still has a high discrimination ability (ICr/I > 2) for Cr(VI), which makes the N-GCC-2 composite feasible for practical applications.

It is known from the above PEC detection that N-GCC composites, in terms of precision, can achieve higher detection sensitivity due to their multi-defect structure and efficient photoelectric conversion properties; in terms of speed, the structure of the material helps to accelerate the separation and transport of photogenerated charges, thus improving the detection efficiency. Also, the multi-defect structures may have tunability in wavelength-selective photoresponsivity through electrical gating. In addition, the synthesis cost of N-GCC is more competitive, and the workability and environmental friendliness are relatively good. Therefore, the composite may show superior performance in dealing with Cr(VI) pollution.

The scavenging experiments of N-GCC-2 were conducted employing methanol, isopropanol, p-benzoquinone, and potassium bromate as quenchers for ·OH, h+, superoxide radicals (·O2 −), and e−, respectively (Fig. S6a). The removal efficiency of Cr(VI) was around 86.47 % due to the use of methanol as a scavenger for ·OH. The inclusion of additional trapping agents did not significantly affect the removal of Cr(VI).Hence, ·OH is the primary reactant in Cr(VI) degradation, as indicated by the Mott-Schottky curves for various materials. Depicted in Fig. S2, the increase in the loading of N-GQDs, leads to its CB gradually shift towards the positive direction. In other word, the energy level of the electrons of N-GCC-2 became higher, resulting in a stronger redox capacity under the light, and therefore able to produce more ·OH. The Cr(VI) removal efficacy of N-GCC-2 was evaluated over multiple cycles (Fig. S6b). After 50 min of light irradiation, the first application of N-GCC-2 showed 95.86 % removal of Cr(VI).After taking part in the reaction, the N-GCC-2 was dried and centrifuged before being added to new Cr(VI) solution for the following phase. After five cycles, N-GCC-2's photocatalytic activity is still strong, and at 120 mg/L, the elimination rate of Cr(VI) is more than 80.00 %. Such a test implies that the introduction of the appropriate amount of defects and the formation of composite materials could change the energy band structure and electronic state of the materials, reduce the photo corrosion of the photocatalytic materials, and improve their photostability.

This work suggests a potential method for the photoreduction of Cr(VI) based on the schematic design of photocatalytic reduction presented in Fig. 8 . Upon exposure to visible light, e− in the VB of CuS, N-GQDs, and Cv-CNNs are photoexcited to the CB, concomitantly generating an equivalent quantity of h+ within the corresponding VBs. N-GQDs' charge distribution and electron trapping properties stabilized the photogenerated e− in the CB of CuS and Cv-CNNs. Concurrently, the robust electrical conductivity of the N-GCC heterostructure (Fig. 4) facilitated the migration of h+ from the VBs of Cv-CNNs and CuS to the aqueous pollutant medium. This process delays e−-h+ recombination, allowing h+ to react with OH− and create ·OH. The N-CQD system successfully separated charges within the conduction band, allowing for the generation of ·O2 − upon interaction with dissolved oxygen in the Cr(VI) aqueous solution. The solution's acidic conditions encourage the conversion of O2 to hydrogen peroxide radicals (HO2·), which then spontaneously decompose to create O2 and ·OH. Following the generation of ·OH, the Cr(VI) that had accumulated on the N-GCC matrix underwent a reduction process facilitated by these ·OH. The proposed mechanism is shown in reaction eqs. (4)–(9):
(4)
N − GQDs / CuS / Cv − CNNs − 70 − 2 + h v → N − GQDs / CuS / Cv − CNNs − 70 − 2
(
e −
+
h +
)
(5)
h +
+
O H
−
→ · O H
(6)
O 2
+
e −
→ · O H
(7)
· O
2 −
+
H +
→
H O
2
·
(8)
H O
2
· → · O H +
O 2
(9)
C r
2
O 7
2 −
+ 14 · O H → 2
C r
3 +
+ 7
H 2
O

In this study, we prepared a N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs photocatalyst with multifocal defects by designing a multifocal defect mechanism, heterojunction materials, and the advantages of photo responsive materials. By taking advantage of the synergistic effects of different types of focal defects and the heterojunction material itself, the adsorption capacity and photocatalytic performance of the material were regulated, and its photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) was accelerated. In addition, the multiple point defects greatly improved the light absorption capacity and photosensitivity of N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs, thus realizing the efficient recognition of Cr(VI). Meanwhile, based on their strong adsorption ability, there was an obvious synergistic effect between the adsorption and photocatalysis of N-GQDs/CuS/Cv-CNNs, and the removal rate of high Cr(VI) concentration (120 mg/L) reached 96.37 % after 50 min of visible light irradiation. This study reveals the great potential of multipoint defective structures for the treatment of Cr(VI) heavy metal contamination, which opens up a new way for the remediation of chromium-contaminated environments. However, the precise conditions required in the synthesis process may lead to higher production costs, as well as issues of stability and reproducibility of the material, which is slightly less stable compared to other materials due to the introduction of the multi-defect mechanism, and more difficult to recycle due to the size of the material. In relation to these drawbacks, the current solution technologies are: synthesis stage, optimization of the synthesis process, use of inexpensive raw materials, control of the synthesis conditions, incorporation of stabilizers, and design of suitable defect engineering strategies. In the post-processing stage, thermal or chemical treatment, surface modification or encapsulation. In the application stage, design composites that are easy to recycle and develop efficient recycling techniques, such as preparing hydrogels, 3D printing them, and electrostatic spinning.

In this study, a multi-defect N-GCC composite with high cost-effectiveness and excellent performance was fabricated by an efficient strategy. This material's capacity to detect and remove Cr(VI) is considerably improved by its unusual multi-defect structure. These defects enhance the light absorption capacity, light sensing efficiency, and photoelectric conversion performance of N-GCC, thus optimizing their recognition of Cr(VI). Moreover, the electronic structure of the surfaces of N-GCC was impacted by their multi-defect structure, which also enhanced the electrostatic attraction to Cr(VI) and hastened its removal. The defect structure not only increases the material's light absorption range but also offers extra energy levels that stabilize photogenerated carriers, according to photoelectrochemical analysis and Cr(VI) photoelectrochemical identification.The study's findings not only support the critical role that multi-defect mechanisms play in addressing dangerous Cr(VI) pollution, but they also provide a path for the creation of innovative geo-multifunctional materials that would lessen the damaging effects of industrial activity on the environment.

Yi Ren: Formal analysis, Validation. Luhua Wang: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Hongfang Zhao: Investigation, Methodology. Hongxia Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiaohui Niu: Resources, Software. Li Chen: Resources. Deyi Zhang: Investigation. Haiyan Fan: Visualization. Kunjie Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
